question: In a titration, $10~\text{cm}^3$ of $0.1~\text{mol/dm}^3$ $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$ is exactly neutralized by $27.64~\text{cm}^3$ of $\text{NaOH}$ solution. Calculate the concentration of the $\text{NaOH}$ solution.

A $1.36~\text{g}$ sample of impure solid $\text{NaOH}$ ($M = 40.0~\text{g/mol}$) is dissolved and completely neutralizes $20~\text{cm}^3$ of $\text{HCl}$ solution with unknown concentration. Assuming the impurity does not react, calculate the concentration of the $\text{HCl}$ if the sample was 100% pure.
explanation: Moles of $\text{NaOH} = \frac{1.36}{40.0} = 0.0339~\text{mol}$. Moles of $\text{HCl}$ = Moles of $\text{NaOH} = 0.0339$. $C_{\text{HCl}} = \frac{n}{V} = \frac{0.0339}{0.02} = 1.7~\text{mol/dm}^3$.

Assuming the impurity does not react, calculate the concentration of the $\text{HCl}$ if the sample was 100% pure.
explanation: Moles of $\text{NaOH} = \frac{1.94}{40.0} = 0.0485~\text{mol}$. Moles of $\text{HCl}$ = Moles of $\text{NaOH} = 0.0485$. $C_{\text{HCl}} = \frac{n}{V} = \frac{0.0485}{0.01} = 4.85~\text{mol/dm}^3$.

A $2~\text{g}$ sample of impure solid $\text{NaOH}$ ($M = 40.0~\text{g/mol}$) is dissolved and completely neutralizes $10~\text{cm}^3$ of $\text{HCl}$ solution with unknown concentration. Assuming the impurity does not react, calculate the concentration of the $\text{HCl}$ if the sample was 100% pure.
explanation: Moles of $\text{NaOH} = \frac{2}{40.0} = 0.05~\text{mol}$. Moles of $\text{HCl}$ = Moles of $\text{NaOH} = 0.05$. $C_{\text{HCl}} = \frac{n}{V} = \frac{0.05}{0.01} = 5~\text{mol/dm}^3$.
